fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a shopping cart feature for the storefront project. Basically customers keep losing their cart contents between page loads which is super frustrating and we're getting complaints. The cart needs to stick around for the whole browsing session — similar to how we handled persistence in that user login flow we did before.

Each item in the cart should track how many the customer wants and what price they're paying, and we need the math to be solid — accounting had some issues last quarter with totals being slightly off on invoices so that's a hard requirement this time. Products should be identifiable and if someone picks the same product twice it shouldn't create a duplicate entry, just update it.

We also want a nice readable description for each cart line so customer service can read it out without looking at raw IDs.

On the technical side, keep the cart logic separate from whatever reads/writes to the outside world — we've had issues before where business logic got tangled up with I/O stuff and it made testing a nightmare. We need a test runner script too that can just be pointed at a folder of test cases and runs everything automatically, writing outputs somewhere we can diff them.

Basically model it after the architecture approach we used in the inventory module — small, clean, no over-engineering. Let me know if anything is unclear.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a cart is re-established against the same session (via the 'reopen' operation), the output must begin with the line 'same_cart=yes' before the cart state dump. This signals that the existing cart was resolved rather than a new one created.

The cart state dump must list each line item on its own line in the format: 'item product=<product-id> quantity=<n> unit_price=<price> subtotal=<price>', with line items ordered by ascending product id. After all line items, print 'total=<price>' (sum of subtotals) and then 'count=<n>' (sum of all quantities). All money amounts must be rendered with exactly two decimal places (e.g. 100.00, 3.20, 12.80).

trigger_keywords: how to avoid floating point errors
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All monetary calculations (subtotals, totals) must use exact fixed-point decimal arithmetic — not binary floating-point (float/double). For example, 4 units at 3.20 must yield exactly 12.80, not a floating-point approximation. Use a Decimal or equivalent exact-arithmetic type in the implementation.

Updating a line item replaces its quantity and unit price entirely — it does not add to the existing quantity. After an update, the cart reflects only the new quantity and new unit price. For example, updating a quantity-3 item to quantity-2 at a new price results in a cart with exactly 2 units at the new price, count=2.

trigger_keywords: what does the label operation output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The label operation outputs a single line in the format 'label=<quantity> units of <product-type-name>', where the product-type-name is the class/type name of the product object (e.g. 'User' for a Django User product fixture with id=1). Example: 'label=3 units of User'.

The test runner is invoked via 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' and accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument (default: 'public_test_cases'). For each test case it writes one output file at 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{stem}@{index:03}.txt' containing only the raw stdout from the program, directly comparable against the expected_output field in the JSON case file.

The core cart logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. A separate execution adapter (entry point) is responsible for reading JSON from stdin, translating the command sequence into calls on the cart core, and writing results to stdout. The cart aggregate, line item storage, money computation, and output formatting must each be in distinct, cohesive units.

trigger_keywords: what happens when updating nonexistent item
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Operations on absent line items (e.g. updating or removing a product that has no line item in the cart) must be modeled as explicit, catchable error conditions — not silent no-ops or untyped generic exceptions. The interface must make these failure cases visible and handleable by the caller.

trigger_keywords: how are products defined in input
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JSON input contains a 'products' field which is a list of product IDs (integers). The adapter is responsible for constructing product fixtures from these IDs — in the Django context, product ID 1 corresponds to a Django User object with pk=1, whose type name is 'User'. These fixtures are passed into cart operations as product references.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

show me feature1 session persistence test cases
what are the feature1 session persistence test inputs
give me the feature1_session_persistence test file
what does feature1 session persistence expect

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to how we handled persistence in that user login flow we did before
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Session-scoped cart binding: a cart is attached to a caller-supplied session object on first use; re-establishing against the same session resolves to the same existing cart rather than creating a new one (the 'reopen' operation + 'same_cart=yes' output contract in feature1_session_persistence.json)

context_pointers.vague_reference: model it after the architecture approach we used in the inventory module — small, clean, no over-engineering
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Scale-Driven Code Organization and SOLID constraints from the PRD: one cart aggregate with logically distinct modules for session binding, line-item storage, total/count computation, and output formatting — no inflated framework structure

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the start.md PRD for the precise contract. If something is still unclear after reviewing those, flag it and we can discuss further.

feature1_session_persistence.json.description: A cart is bound to a caller session on first use and persists for the lifetime of that session. Re-establishing a cart against the same session must resolve to the same underlying open cart rather than starting a new one, so its contents survive across separate request cycles.

cases.expected_output:
same_cart=yes
item product=1 quantity=1 unit_price=100.00 subtotal=100.00
total=100.00
count=1

feature2_add_item.json.description: Adding a product places a line item in the cart that references the product and records its quantity and unit price. A freshly added line item is retrievable from the cart with the exact quantity and unit price supplied, and its subtotal equals quantity times unit price.

cases.expected_output:
item product=1 quantity=1 unit_price=100.00 subtotal=100.00
total=100.00
count=1

feature3_subtotal_decimal.json.description: A line item's subtotal is its quantity multiplied by its unit price, computed with exact fixed-point decimal arithmetic. Unit prices may be whole numbers or fractional money amounts, and the subtotal preserves two-decimal monetary precision without floating-point rounding error.

cases.expected_output:
item product=1 quantity=3 unit_price=100.00 subtotal=300.00
total=300.00
count=3

item product=1 quantity=4 unit_price=3.20 subtotal=12.80
total=12.80
count=4

feature4_update_item.json.description: Updating an existing line item replaces its quantity and unit price with the supplied values (it does not accumulate onto the prior quantity). After an update, the cart total reflects the new unit price times the new quantity, and the cart's reported item count equals the new quantity.

cases.expected_output:
item product=1 quantity=2 unit_price=200.00 subtotal=400.00
total=400.00
count=2

feature5_item_label.json.description: A line item carries a human-readable label that combines its quantity with the name of the product type it references, so a cart listing can be rendered without inspecting raw fields.
